Section 18.11
REACTION WITH SECONDARY AMINES: ENAMINES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Nucleophilic Addition of 𝟐 degrees Amines
Secondary amines have no hydrogen to eliminate after addition across C=O

Mechanism of Enamine Formation
As for 1 degree amines, the first steps involve formation of a hemiaminal
The hemiaminal expels water to give a resonance-stabilized iminium ion
Deprotonation at the α -position gives the enamine

Alkenes from Carbonyls
The Wittig reaction involves the synthesis of alkenes from carbonyls and phosphonium ylides
The ylide is, like C=O both nucleophilic and electrophilic
The nucleophilic ylide carbon bonds to the carbonyl carbon, eventually forming C=C

Preparation of Phosphonium Ylides
To prepare phosphonium ylides, phosphines are first reacted with alkyl halides to form the P–C bond …
…then the phosphonium is deprotonated using a strong base, such as the conjugate base of DMSO

Mechanism of the Wittig Reaction
Treatment of the ylide with a carbonyl compound gives a new C=C bond , usually with trans configuration where applicable
This reaction is driven by the great strength of the P=O bond
The mechanism involves “cycloaddition” followed by “retro-cycloaddition”

Section 18.13
STEREOSELECTIVE ADDITION TO CARBONYL GROUPS

Diastereoselective Addition
Chiral ketones and aldehydes have two distinct “faces” above and below the carbonyl group
Addition usually occurs selectively at the less hindered face
This sense of addition is both kinetically and thermodynamically favored

Enzymatic Enantioselective Addition
Enzymes catalyze the enantioselective addition of nucleophiles to carbonyl groups
The chiral environment of the enzyme shields one face from nucleophilic attack

Aldehydes to Carboxylic Acids
Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylic acids by chromic acid, H 2 CrO 4 (and conditions that generate it)
The hydrated aldehyde (geminal diol) is likely involved
Because the hydrate is more reactive than an alcohol, any aqueous oxidation conditions will convert RCH 2 OH to RCO 2 H

Biological Oxidation
Aldehydes are converted to carboxylic acids by aldehyde dehydrogenase enzymes
NAD + acts as a hydride acceptor in these reactions; water is the source of – OH
The body uses a dehydrogenase to convert toxic acetaldehyde into harmless acetic acid

Section 18.15
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES

Infrared Spectroscopy
Aldehydes and ketones give a very strong, characteristic peak between 1710 – 1750 cm( − 1)
Aldehyde C–H stretch also appears in spectra of aldehydes (2720, 2820 cm( − 1)

1 H NMR
The aldehyde proton is highly deshielded ( δ 9 – 10) and very characteristic
α -Protons in aldehydes and ketones are deshielded relative to alkane protons ( δ 2 – 3)

13 C NMR
The carbonyl carbon appears far downfield in 13 C NMR
α -Carbons are generally deshielded somewhat relative to alkane carbons

UV-Vis Spectroscopy
The C=O group has two important excitations in the ultraviolet region
Aldehydes and ketones are usually transparent in the absence of additional conjugation

Mass Spectrometry
Molecular ion peaks are generally prominent
Loss of an alkyl radical from the molecular radical cation gives a stabilized acylium ion
